content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 10, 1987

Harry R. Peacock
City Clerk
City of Saratoga
13777 Fruitvale Avenue
Saratoga, CA 95070

Re: Your Request for Assistance
Our File No. I-87-284

Dear Mr. Peacock:

This is in reply to your letter concerning a local
committee, "Protect our Environment." I spoke to Grace Cory of your
office, and Ms. Cory provided me with additional information.

Also, Joan Hershkowitz, chairperson of the committee, called
me and provided additional information. Ms. Hershkowitz informed me
that the the committee is no longer active and does not anticipate
any activity in the near future. If the committee becomes involved
in making campaign contributions or expenditures, the committee's
statement of organization (Form 410) will be amended to reflect the
change in Part IV or Part V of the statement of organization. In the
meantime, the committee will file the mandatory semi-annual
statements required of all recipient committees.

Ms. Hershkowitz also indicated that the organization has
received, and anticipates continued receipt of, monetary donations
for the purpose of financing a legal challenge in connection with
proposed State Highway 85.

Ms. Hershkowitz understands that funds received and spent
for this litigation are not reportable "contributions" and
"expenditures," and to the extent the money is not deposited into the
campaign committee account, such receipts and expenditures will not
be reported on the campaign statements filed by Protect our
Environment.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Harry R. Peacock
December 10, 1987
Page Two


          Please contact me if you have any questions about this
letter, or if you would like any additional information.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Jeanne Pritchard
                                        Division Chief
                                        Technical Assistance and
                                          Analysis Division

cc:  Joan Hershkowitz, Chairperson,
     Protect Our Environment

CITY of SARATOGA
13777 FRUITVALE AVENUE, SARATOGA CALIFORNIA 95070
(408) 867-3438
COUNCIL MEMBERS:
Karen Anderson
Martha Clevenger
Joyce Hlava
David Moyles
Donald Peterson

November 6, 1987

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Dear Members of the Commission:

In 1986 a local political action committee, Protect Our Environment, was formed
to support a ballot measure and three candidates for election to the City
Council in the general municipal election in June 1986. On May 12, 1986, they
filed a Statement of Organization (Recipient Committee) with the Secretary of
State.

In the past few weeks this committee has been attempting to raise money to
litigate the Final Environmental Impact Statement for State Route 85 and in their literature have implied rather directly their intention to support
their candidates for office in the June 1988 election. Copies of the Committee's
Statement of Organization and relevant letters to the editor, etc. are enclosed
to verify the above statements.

I have written to the Treasurer of the Committee suggesting the committee
contact the Commission to determine what further filings this committee may be
required to make in order to meet the requirements of the law and your
Commission's regulations. I would appreciate any advice you could give me on
this matter.

Sincerely,

Harry R. Peacock
City Clerk

Enclosures: Protect Our Environment Statement of Organization
Letter to the Editor, Saratoga News, 11/4/87
Letter from Joan Hershkowitz

II IS THIS A CONTROLLED COMMITTEE?

A controlled committee is one which is controlled directly or indirectly by a candidate or a proponent of a state ballot measure or which acts jointly with a candidate, controlled committee or proponent of a state ballot measure in connection with the making of expenditures. A candidate or proponent of a state ballot measure controls a committee if he or she, his or her agent, or any other committee(s) he or she controls, has a significant influence on the actions or decisions of the committee.)

VERIFICATION

I have used all reasonable diligence in preparing this Statement. I have reviewed the Statement and to the best of my knowledge and belief, the information contained herein is true and complete.

I certify under penalty of perjury under the laws of the State of California that the foregoing is true and correct.

Executed on _5-8-86_ at _Saratoga, Calif._ by ________________
                 (Date)        (City and State)        (Signature of Treasurer)

Action must be taken to challenge Hwy 85

EDITOR:
On Oct. 15 the State Highway Commission accepted the Final Environmental Impact Statement (FEIS) for Route 85. The only alternative analyzed in depth is a roller-coaster freeway with three off-ramps in Saratoga instead of the Saratoga Design Variation which was to have been a fully depressed freeway with no off-ramps in Saratoga. A

large part of the city will be bisected with an overhead or street level freeway and over-head interchanges.

This could well be our last chance to challenge this freeway alternative, but action must be taken immediately. The challenge must be submitted before Nov. 12.

Examples of deficiencies include:
• No honest traffic study which describes resulting impaction of Lawrence Expressway and Saratoga-Sunnyvale Roads from south San Jose, Almaden Valley & Coyote Valley residents using the freeway onto our routes to get to the job centers of the county, thereby causing an effective loss of capacity for the Saratogans' primary

routes. The FEIS fails, also, to report the air quality losses due to the impaction on Lawrence and Saratoga-Sunnyvale Roads.
• According to the FEIS one-third of the current Route 101 trips will be diverted onto Route 85, and virtually all will exit onto Route 87, a road assumed made even more so now that current construction has been halted due to the discovery of

very high levels of asbestos on Route 87.
• The FEIS reports substantial growth impacts in Coyote Valley, which result directly from the construction of a freeway, while ignoring the subject of air-quality loss due to the newly generated traffic. These and other grossly defective assumptions lead to a significant understatement of the air pollution potential caused by the freeway.
• Only one alternative is chosen. One studied alternative is inadequate to meet the requirements of the law. The no-project alternative, a requirement under the California Environmental Quality Act, was rejected and virtually no study.
• There is reason to believe that soils in parts of the Route 85 Corridor are heavily laden with asbestos and they are in the Route 87 Corridor. This issue was not studied although the health of half of our citizens may be at risk.
• The cancer risk to children resulting from the proposed shifting of the power lines closer to houses was not analyzed at all.
• These defects mean that the brakes must be applied to the process so that accurate effects can be revealed. Perhaps in time for more environmentally-concerned decision-makers to be elected in June of 1988.

85 is counterproductive use of tax dollars

EDITOR:
Jack Christian makes a not-so-veiled threat of "no mitigation" to people who challenge the single-alternative Environmental Impact Statement for a Route 85 freeway. With only a single-alternative to choose from, what could he possibly take away?

Like the Route 87 neighbors, he would have us see the so called "depressed freeway" rising above us before we are aware of plans for it, and to stop it. Under his threat of "no mitigation" he would talk us into Almaden Valley or to the Area Junior College. Not many of us need go these places. Few would really want a Route 101 by-pass in our city.

A Route 85 freeway is an entirely counterproductive use of our tax dollars. There are serious deficiencies in routes which must travel every day such as Saratoga-Sunnyvale, Lawrence Expressway, and Route 17.

The Route 85 freeway will increase congestion on these facilities while consuming the money which may have improved them. Most people in Saratoga and Los Gatos will be

except a rollercoaster, three Saratoga interchange freeway. He seems to accept anything, no matter how irresponsible to be allowed to hurry on down to Almaden Valley or to the Area Junior College. Not many of us need go these places. Few would really want a Route 101 by-pass in our city.

A Route 85 freeway is an entirely counterproductive use of our tax dollars. There are serious deficiencies in routes which must travel every day such as Saratoga-Sunnyvale, Lawrence Expressway, and Route 17.

The Route 85 freeway will increase congestion on these facilities while consuming the money which may have improved them. Most people in Saratoga and Los Gatos will be

hurt by a freeway, aggravated by each Saratoga interchange and by overhead and at-grade construction.

Delay is necessary so the law can be followed, so decision makers have available analysis of an array of proposals, and so reason can prevail. Nothing will be lost by taking time for the truth.

People can contribute to make possible a challenge to the EIS by only a few days remain before the challenge must be filed. Please contact Protect Our Environment, P.O. Box 2371, Saratoga, 95070. All money collected will be returned if not enough is raised for the challenge.

Suzanne Jensen
Saratoga

LETTERS TO THE EDITOR

Please keep letters brief and preferably typewritten and double spaced. Readers are invited to write on any topic. However, personal attacks and letters in poor taste will not be published.

All letters must be signed. Anonymous letters and form letters will not be printed.

Send your letters to: Editor, 12145 Saratoga-Sunnyvale Road, Suite 8, Saratoga, CA 95070.

PRE-HOLIDAY SALE

FALL FOR HOME ESTIMATES!

40 to
60%
off

LORDS INSURANCE AGENCY INC.
AFFILIATED WITH ATLANTIC PACIFIC INSURANCE BROKERS, INC.

Ted DuPont (408) 559-4140        John H. Laws

Verification of Auto Insurance
is NOW mandatory!

Fast Quotes
Friendly Service

3190 S. BASCOM AVE., SUITE 270, SAN JOSE, CA 95124

We are suggesting a contribution of $100 from each and every household. This money must be raised at once. If the amount collected is not enough to proceed with the legal challenge, the funds will be returned to each contributor.

Please make checks payable to: Protect Our Environment, POB 2371, Saratoga 95070.

As your neighbor I am most grateful for your help.

Joan Herskowitz
Chairperson, Protect
Our Environment

PROTECT OUR ENVIRONMENT   !!!

The Saratoga City Council by unanimous vote informed the County
Traffic Authority on January 17, 1986 that, regarding the proposed Highway
85 freeway, this city wanted, among other things, a fully depressed
freeway with no interchanges in this city. Protect Our Environment supports
this position. The council also, then and since, requested certain
environmental concerns be addressed. POE agrees there is an urgent need
for these. The Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) by Caltrans did not
answer any of the council's requests. For these, and other reasons, POE
feels that the EIS does not meet the requirements of the Federal and State
laws regarding environmental impacts.

The city council, by letter dated September 17, 1987 to the Santa
Clara County Traffic Authority, California Department of Transportation,
and Federal Highway Administration strongly protested. This letter was
approved unanimously by the four council members present, and was signed
by the Mayor. POE agrees with the gist of this letter, but also feels that
to ensure the needs of Saratoga are met, action must be taken to legally
challenge the sufficiency of the EIS.

As stated in the final EIS by Caltrans, the freeway through Saratoga
would be elevated over Prospect Ave. and Quito Road, and would be at
grade over a depressed Saratoga Ave. In between the freeway would be at
grade or partially depressed. There would be interchanges at Prospect
Avenue, Saratoga Avenue, and Quito Road. This EIS was approved by the
State Highway Commission on October 15, 1987 in spite of the City Council's
letter, and over the objections of POE's attorney.

POE has retained an attorney to challenge the EIS. It will be expensive,
so funds must be solicited. To accomplish this, volunteers will be contacting
homeowners to donate the funds necessary to finance the legal challenge
to halt the process until the intent, as well as the letter, of the law is met.

Chairperson
Protect Our Environment

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 9, 1987

Harry R. Peacock
City Clerk
13777 Fruitvale Avenue
Saratoga, CA 95070

Re: 87-284

Dear Mr. Peacock:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on November 9, 1987 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5662.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written
advice. If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to
advise you as to the information needed. If your request is
for informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we
can. (See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Adm. Code Sec.
18329).)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Jeanne Pritchard
Chief
Technical Assistance and Analysis
Division

JP:plh

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

CITY of SARATOGA
13777 FRUITVALE AVENUE • SARATOGA, CALIFORNIA 95070
(408) 867-3438

COUNCIL MEMBERS:
Karen Anderson
Martha Cievenger
Joyce Hiava
David Moyes
Donald Peterson

November 6, 1987

Charlene Low, Treasurer
Protect Our Environment
19756 Solana Drive
Saratoga, California 95070

Subject: Protect Our Environment - Litigation Activity

Dear Ms. Low:

I note from recent letters to the editor of the Saratoga News dated November 4, 1987, and letters being circulated in the community that your organization is attempting to raise money to litigate the adequacy of the accepted FEIS for Route 85.

A check of your organization's Statement of Organization filed with the Secretary of State on May 12, 1986, does not list this activity as one of the intended purposes of the organization, which was to Support Measure B and candidates Cole Bridges, Karen Anderson and Phil Stokes in the 1986 municipal election. Given the fact that the election has now been over for some time, and the fact that you now appear to be engaged in new types of activities, I would suggest that you consult either with competent legal counsel or with the Fair Political Practices Commission on actions you may be required to initiate in order to comply with California law regarding your activities.

For assistance in contacting the Commission staff, please feel free to contact Grace E. Cory, Deputy City Clerk, here at City Hall.

Sincerely,

Harry R. Peacock
City Clerk

cc: Fair Political Practices Commission
    City Council

sections.question: In 1986 a local political action committee, Protect Our Environment, was formed to support a ballot measure and three candidates for election to the City Council in the general municipal election in June 1986. On May 12, 1986, they filed a Statement of Organization (Recipient Committee) with the Secretary of State. In the past few weeks this committee has been attempting to raise money to litigate the Final Environmental Impact Statement for State Route 85 and in their literature have implied rather directly their intention to support candidates for office in the June 1988 election. What further filings may this committee be required to make in order to meet the requirements of the law and the Commission's regulations?
sections.conclusion: The committee is no longer active and does not anticipate activity in the near future. If it becomes involved in making campaign contributions or expenditures, the statement of organization will be amended. In the meantime, the committee will file mandatory semi-annual statements as a recipient committee. Funds received and spent for litigation are not reportable 'contributions' and 'expenditures,' and to the extent the money is not deposited in the campaign account, such receipts and expenditures will not be reported on campaign statements.

Division 


November 6, 1987 
-0807 
Dear ~~rs of the Commission: 
In 1986 a local political action conunittee, Protect Our Environment, was formed 
to support a ballot measure and three candidates for election to the City 
Council in the general municipal election in June 1986. 
On May 12, 1986, they 
filed a Statement of Organization (Recipient Conunittee) with the Secretary of 
State. 
In the past few weeks this committee has been attempting to raise money to 
litigate the Final Environmental Impact Statement for State Route 85 and in 
their literature have implied rather directly their intention to support 
candidates for office in the June 1988 election. Conies of the Committee's 
Statement of Organization and relevant letters to the editor, etc. are enclosed 
to verify the above statements. 
I have written to the Treasurer of the Committee suggesting the committee 
contact the Conunission to determine what further filings this conunittee may be 
required to make in order to meet the requirements of the law and your 
Conunisson's regulations. 
I would appreciate any advice you could give me on 
this matter.
sections.question_synthetic: What reporting and filing requirements apply to a political committee that was originally formed to support candidates and a ballot measure, but is now primarily raising funds for environmental litigation while also indicating possible future support for 1988 candidates?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised that litigation-related funds are not reportable as campaign contributions or expenditures if kept separate from campaign accounts, and the committee must file mandatory semi-annual statements while it remains registered, with amendments if future campaign activity resumes.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (November 6, 1987 letter from Harry Peacock) and FPPC response (December 10, 1987 letter from Jeanne Pritchard). Also includes committee statement of organization and related newspaper letters to editor. OCR quality is good but some formatting issues present. Extracted primary response content.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: In 1986 a local political action committee, Protect Our Environment, was formed to support a ballot measure and three candidates for election to the City Council in the general municipal election in June 1986. On May 12, 1986, they filed a Statement of Organization (Recipient Committee) with the Secretary of State. In the past few weeks this committee has been attempting to raise money to litigate the Final Environmental Impact Statement for State Route 85 and in their literature have implied rather directly their intention to support candidates for office in the June 1988 election. What further filings may this committee be required to make in order to meet the requirements of the law and the Commission's regulations?

CONCLUSION: The committee is no longer active and does not anticipate activity in the near future. If it becomes involved in making campaign contributions or expenditures, the statement of organization will be amended. In the meantime, the committee will file mandatory semi-annual statements as a recipient committee. Funds received and spent for litigation are not reportable 'contributions' and 'expenditures,' and to the extent the money is not deposited in the campaign account, such receipts and expenditures will not be reported on campaign statements.

embedding.summary: FPPC response to City Clerk regarding reporting obligations of 'Protect Our Environment,' a local committee that originally supported 1986 candidates and measures but is now primarily fundraising for environmental litigation while hinting at future candidate support in 1988. The FPPC clarified that litigation-related funds are not reportable campaign activity if kept separate.
